course_description:
This capstone course examines how international business strategy aligns with organisational policy and strategic ambitions. Emphasising theory-driven analysis, it explores the organisational and institutional factors shaping global strategy. Students develop intellectual leadership by undertaking a substantial research project addressing key challenges and opportunities in international business.

This capstone course explores international business strategy through a theory-driven lens, focusing on how firms navigate diverse institutional environments. These environments include formal actors, such as governments and transnational agencies, as well as informal institutions and market dynamics that shape strategic choices. The course introduces students to key theoretical frameworks - including internationalisation process theory, the eclectic paradigm, transaction cost economics, institutional theory, resource and knowledge-based views, and global value chain theory - to understand how international strategies are formed, implemented, and challenged. International business is inherently transdisciplinary, and the course draws on cognate fields to enrich analysis. Students are expected to take intellectual initiative by managing components of the course delivery and leading an independent research project. The course equips students to critically engage with complex global challenges and to develop theory-informed insights relevant to international firms of all sizes.

restrictions: Capstone course to be studied in the major. Course is restricted to students enrolled in the BAdvBus(Hons) program.

course_aims:
In this course, you will acquire the following knowledge and skills:

• Scholarly content knowledge: knowledge of concepts, theories and approaches at research in IB.

• Theoretical application: the ability to translate IB theory into strategy for international firms.

• Analytical proficiency: the ability to apply content knowledge to address research questions and issues at the firm-level on cross national border activities.

• Critical reasoning: the ability to critically evaluate research in IB.

• Communication proficiency: the ability to present influential reviews, proposals and research insights.

• Research ethics: the ability to conduct research with academic integrity, including proper attribution of sources, ethical handling of information, and respectful engagement in scholarly dialogue.

This learning will position you academically for future studies in higher degrees and in corporate and government enterprise.

learning_outcomes.description: Articulate the relevance and application of IB scholarship to engage and influence various stakeholders.
Critically evaluate IB research and theories for its relevance, impact, and strategic implications.
Propose a valuable and viable research project to address an impactful research question or puzzle.
Critically comply with principles of ethical responsibility as determined by the Academy of International Business and The University of Queensland.

assessment_summary.title: Discussion: Leadership, Moderation, Participation
assessment_summary.due_date: Block 1: Week 2 - Week 3; Block 2: Week 4 - Week 5; Block 3: Week 6 - Week 7; Block 4: Week 8 - Week 9; Block 5: Week 10 - Week 11; Block 6: Week 12 - Week 13; Review due prior to discussion session of each Block

assessment_details.title: Research Proposal Development
assessment_details.task_description: Each student is required to write a research proposal on an International Business (IB) topic. You will not conduct the actual project.You will focus on a puzzle or a research question in IB that interests you, and you will structure a full proposal on how you would conduct this research project, should you ever engage in it.The research may be theoretical or empirical in nature, but should make a clear novel contribution to the international business literature.AI StatementArtificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance.A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.Read full task descriptionHide full task description

assessment_details.task_description: This course is structured into six blocks of two weeks with theory presented in the first week and application of theory to firm strategy following in the second week.The first week of each block will be presented by the Lecturer in this course.In the second week, the class activity will be guided by student participation with oversight by the Lecturer.In each of these six weeks, a student (or more than one, depending on enrolment numbers) will direct the class activity as the class draws strategy implications from the theory presented in the previous week.Students will be peer-assessed in each of these six group sessions.In addition, the Lecturer will assess each student according to participation, insight and final strategy outcomes.To prepare for the class discussion, each student will submit a 400 word review of one manuscript recommended on the topic.The Lecturer will assess each manuscript submitted.AI StatementArtificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance.A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.Read full task descriptionHide full task description

learning_activities.topic: Introduction to and Evolution of the IB fieldIn this first seminar, we will explain the international business (IB) research field, and discuss the development and evolution of this field. We also discuss the meaning and importance of theory and its application.Learning outcomes:L01

Internationalisation Process TheoryWe introduce a core theory in IB - The Uppsala Internationalisation process model. In the 1970s, Swedish scholars from The University of Uppsala first introduced the internationalisation process model which has been elaborated regularly. We focus on discussing two papers, the original published in 1977 and a more recent one.Learning outcomes:L02, L03, L04
Strategy Implications of IP theoryStudent led group discussion. There are two strategy papers based on IP theory for students to discuss.Learning outcomes:L02, L03, L04

Internalisation theory and Eclectic ParadigmIn this seminar, we focus on theory of the MNE and the Eclectic Paradigm of International Production. The Eclectic Paradigm explains why firms undertake production across borders to become MNEs.Learning outcomes:L02, L03, L04
Strategy Implications of Internalisation TheoryStudent led group discussion. There are two strategy papers based on internalisation theory for students to discuss.Learning outcomes:L02, L03, L04

Transaction Cost TheoryThe third theory we introduce is Williamsonian TCE. TCE is a classic economics theory which has been widely used across different fields. We discuss two papers from Williamson.Learning outcomes:L02, L03, L04
Strategy Implications of Transaction Cost TheoryStudent led group discussion. There are two strategy papers based on TCE theory for students to discuss.Learning outcomes:L02, L03, L04

Institutional TheoryInstitutional theory focuses on the importance of context and the environment which are key in IB. Institutional theory emphasises institutions directly shaping firms' strategies. We will discuss various aspects of institutions and how these impact firm behaviours.Learning outcomes:L02, L03, L04
Strategy Implications of Institutional TheoryStudent led group discussion. There are two strategy papers based on institutional theory for students to discuss.Learning outcomes:L02, L03, L04

Resource-Based View - Dynamic Capabilities ViewThe resource-based view and dynamic capabilities emphasise firm heterogeneity. Firms' advantages and performance vary even when they are in the same industry and the same institutional environment. We will discuss the key assumptions and logics from RBV and the dynamic capabilities view.Kings Birthday Public Holiday - Monday 6 October 2025- Check Blackboard for announcements about affected classes.Learning outcomes:L02, L03, L04
Strategy Implications of RBV - Dynamic CapabilitesStudent led group discussion. There are two strategy papers based on resource-based view for students to discuss.Learning outcomes:L02, L03, L04

Global Value Chain TheoryThe Global Factory and Externalisation. It is critical for firms to utilise location advantages and spread risks through building global value chain participation. We will discuss the global value chain perspective and how MNEs build and manage participation in global value chains.Learning outcomes:L02, L03, L04
Strategy Implications of Global Value Chain TheoryStudent led group discussion. There are two strategy papers based on global value chain for students to discuss.Learning outcomes:L02, L03, L04
